comments.text: Heuristically this is not true by Cramer like conjecture. Let $d_n=p_{n+1}-p_n$ be prime gaps and define $M(x):=\max_{n<x} d_n$ and $P(x):=\max_{n<x} d_{n-1}d_n$. In a Cramer-Poisson heuristic one treats the normalized gaps\[ X_n:=\frac{d_n}{\log p_n} \]as approximately i.i.d. exponential random variables with mean $1$ (locally, on the scale of $\log p_n$), and also approximately independent for neighboring indices. For $N$ i.i.d. $\mathrm{Exp}(1)$ variables, the maximum satisfies $X_{(1)}=\max_{1\le n\le N}X_n=(1+o(1))\log N$ with high probability. Translating back, this predicts\[ M(x)\approx (\log p_x)\,\log x, \]up to lower-order factors, consistent with the usual extreme-value behavior in the model. Consider adjacent pairs $(X_n,X_{n-1})$ and set a threshold $t=a\log N$. Under independence,\[ \mathbb{E}\#\{1\le n\le N:\; X_{n-1}\ge t,\ X_n\ge t\} \approx N\,\mathbb{P}(X\ge t)^2 = N e^{-2t} = N^{\,1-2a}. \]Hence for any $a<\tfrac12$ there are typically many indices with $X_{n-1},X_n\ge a\log N$, while for $a>\tfrac12$ there are typically none; this places the largest adjacent pair around $t\approx \tfrac12\log N$ in each coordinate. Consequently,\[ \max_{n\le N} X_{n-1}X_n \approx \left(\tfrac12 \log N\right)^2 \quad\text{while}\quad \left(\max_{n\le N}X_n\right)^2 \approx (\log N)^2, \]so the ratio $P(x)/M(x)^2$ is heuristically bounded away from $0$ (on the order of a positive constant, plausibly about $1/4$ in this simplified model), rather than tending to $0$.
Related OEIS: The numerator is the record version of A083550 and the denominator the square of A005250. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
